title: switch
subtitle: Pick one path out of many — without a giant pile of if-else.
goal: Use the classic switch statement (case, break, default), avoid the fall-through trap, and write modern switch expressions with the arrow (->).

blocks.heading: Why switch? 🔀
blocks.html:
Imagine a vending machine. You press a button and it gives you exactly one thing: button 1 → chips, button 2 → soda, button 3 → candy. That's what switch does. It looks at one value and jumps straight to the matching choice.

You could do this with a long chain of if / else if, but when you're comparing the same variable over and over, switch is cleaner and easier to read.

blocks.heading: The classic switch statement
blocks.html:
A switch needs three things:

  • case — a value to match, like case 3:
  • break — says "I'm done, jump out of the switch."
  • default — runs when nothing else matched (like an "else").

Below we switch on day, which is hardcoded to 3.

blocks.caption: Turn a number into a day name
blocks.explain: day is 3, so Java jumps to case 3, prints Wednesday, then break stops it. The other cases and default are skipped.

blocks.html: Don't forget break! If you leave it out, Java keeps running the NEXT cases too — even though they didn't match. This sneaky bug is called fall-through. See it below before you trust your code.

blocks.caption: Oops — no break means fall-through
blocks.explain: light is 1, so it matches case 1 and prints YELLOW. But with no break, it falls through into case 2 and prints GREEN too! That's almost never what you want.

blocks.question: What does break do inside a switch?
options: It jumps out of the switch so the next cases don't run
blocks.explain: break stops the switch right there. Without it, Java falls through into the cases below.

blocks.heading: Modern switch expressions: the arrow → 🏹
blocks.html:
Newer Java (version 14 and up) added a cleaner style. Instead of case 1: with a break, you write case 1 ->. The arrow means "do exactly this, no fall-through, no break needed."

Even better: a switch expression can hand you back a value you store in a variable. If a case needs several lines, wrap them in { } and use yield to give back the answer.

blocks.caption: A menu that gives back a price
blocks.explain: Notice switch is on the RIGHT of the = sign — it produces a value. choice is "pizza", so price becomes "$8". Each case uses -> so no break is needed. You can switch on a String too, not just numbers!

blocks.html: You can switch on an int, a String, or an enum (a fixed list of named choices you'll meet later). For multi-line cases, use case X -> { ... yield value; } to give back the result.

blocks.question: What's the big advantage of the arrow style (case X ->) over the old colon style?
options: You don't need break — there's no accidental fall-through
blocks.explain: With ->, each case runs only its own code. No break, no fall-through bug.

blocks.question: When is switch a better choice than a long if-else chain?
options: When you're comparing the SAME variable against many possible values
When you never want to compare anything
Only when the value is exactly 7
blocks.explain: switch shines when one variable is checked against several fixed values. For ranges or different conditions, if-else still wins.

blocks.title: Name the day
blocks.instructions: The variable day is set to 5. Add a case so the program prints Friday. Use the classic style with break. Then press Check.
blocks.passMsg: Friday it is — your switch jumped to the right case!
blocks.failMsg: Add case 5: that prints Friday, and don't forget break;

blocks.title: Traffic light decider
blocks.instructions: Finish the modern switch expression. light is "GREEN", so action should become "Go!". Add the GREEN case using the arrow -> style. Then press Check.
blocks.passMsg: Green means Go! Your switch expression works perfectly.

challenge.title: Build a meal-picker menu
challenge.instructions: Use a modern switch expression to turn a menu number into a meal name. choice is set to 2, which should be "Tacos". Fill in cases 1, 2, and 3 using the arrow -> style, then print two lines: the option picked and the meal.
challenge.solutionOutput:
You picked option 2
Enjoy your Tacos!
challenge.hint: Each line looks like: case 2 -> "Tacos"; — the arrow points to the value, and the whole switch becomes the value of meal.
challenge.passMsg: 🏆 Order up! You built a real menu with a switch expression — Day 7 complete!
